elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models are trained on massive scrapes of the web, as required by current scaling laws.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most progress is made for English, given its abundance of high-quality pretraining data.
elements.sent-2411-003: For most other languages, however, such high quality pretraining data is unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we study how to boost pretrained model performance in a data constrained target language by enlisting data from an auxiliary language for which high quality data is available.
elements.sent-2411-005: We study this by quantifying the performance gap between training with data in a data-rich auxiliary language compared with training in the target language, exploring the benefits of translation systems, studying the limitations of model scaling for data constrained languages, and proposing new methods for upsampling data from the auxiliary language.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results show that stronger auxiliary datasets result in performance gains without modification to the model or training objective for close languages, and, in particular, that performance gains due to the development of more information-rich English pretraining datasets can extend to targeted language settings with limited data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance on many tasks, including mathematical reasoning, coding capabilities, and knowledge-based question answering~\citep{brown2020language,bubeck2023sparks, OpenAI2023GPT4TR through pretraining on large corpora of web text.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The vast majority of LLMs have been trained and evaluated on English, motivated by the abundance of high quality data.
elements.sent-2411-009: For other languages such large quantities of high quality and information-rich data are unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-010: Consequently, breakthroughs of similar scale are lacking for other languages, due to the lack of good data for pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-011: Most non-English progress comes from relatively small bilingual models~\cite[e.g.,][]{le2019flaubert, de2019bertje, martin2019camembert, scheible2020gottbert, wei2023skywork, faysse2024croissantllm , or larger massively multilingual models~\cite[e.g.,][]{le2023bloom, intrator-etal-2024-breaking, ustun-etal-2024-aya .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Other LLMs such as Llama-2, GPT-3, and PaLM-2 that perform well across a variety of languages are trained primarily on English data with less than 20\% of their data coming from other languages \cite{xu2024survey .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, little progress has been made in understanding when an auxiliary language (such as English) can help learning a target language.
elements.sent-2411-014: This is particularly relevant to understand in the case of target languages for which limited data is available.

elements.sent-2411-015: This work studies the challenge of building datasets for pretraining language models with limited target language data.
elements.sent-2411-016: While much of the progress on building datasets for language model pretraining has focused on collecting and filtering more data , typically English (Figure~\ref{fig:data_pipeline ), we investigate whether these advances transfer implicitly to auxiliary-target language learning (Figure~\ref{fig:aux_pretrain ), where English becomes an auxiliary language.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, this work investigates whether better English datasets also lead to better models in other languages.
elements.sent-2411-018: We focus on the impact of dataset size, filtering for data quality and style, and data selection for specialized information relevant to downstream evaluations (Figure~\ref{fig:data_transforms ), matching recent advancements in state-of-the-art open-source English datasets such as FineWeb-EDU \cite{penedo2024fineweb , and DataComp \cite{li2024datacomp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Auxiliary English data that is generated by some of the existing model-based data filtering pipelines for English can be helpful to complement limited data in a target language (Section~\ref{sec:dataset_comparisons );.}
elements.sent-2411-021: When training with higher quality auxiliary data, more gains can be attributed to relevant information in the auxiliary data (Section~\ref{sec:mb_filter -\ref{sec:is_information );.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Findings are not the same across multiple languages.
elements.sent-2411-023: We hypothesize that for languages that are ``far'' from English, gains from better English datasets do not help (Section~\ref{sec:multilingual );.}
elements.sent-2411-024: There are limits to the size of models that are practical to pretrain with limited target data.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is because data size should scale linearly with model size \cite{kaplan2020scaling and performance in the target language saturates without increasing target data regardless of increasing auxiliary data (Section~\ref{sec:model_size_scaling ).}}

elements.sent-2411-026: While much of the LLM research has focused on English, large-scale transformer-based multilingual language models have been trained on large multilingual corpora including mBERT \cite{pires2019multilingual , XLM \cite{conneau2019cross , mT5 \cite{xue2020mt5 , PolyLM \cite{wei2023polylm , and Bloom \cite{le2023bloom .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: These works focus on training models that are language balanced and can reason in over 100 languages.
elements.sent-2411-028: Other state-of-the-art language models such as Llama 2 \cite{touvron2023llama , Falcon \cite{almazrouei2023falcon , and Palm 2 \cite{anil2023palm have multilingual capabilities but over 90\% the training data is English, and these models are shown to perform poorly across a variety of languages, such as south east Asian languages \cite{nguyen2023seallms .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Other works focus on training smaller bilingual language models in French \cite{faysse2024croissantllm,le2019flaubert,martin2019camembert , German \cite{scheible2020gottbert , Dutch \cite{de2019bertje , or Chinese \cite{wei2023skywork , but require a substantial amount of bilingual data: English and the respective language for pretraining.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Other works focus on understanding languages LLMs reason in \cite{wendler2024llamas , and languages LLMs cannot learn \cite{borenstein2024languages,kallini2024mission .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Still, little work has examined how information seen during pretraining in one language can help down-stream task performance in another language.
elements.sent-2411-032: The survey explains that cross-lingual transfer is a well studied topic for classic NLP tasks, such as part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, dependency parsing, natural language inference, machine translation, etc., although findings are not always consistent.
elements.sent-2411-033: Cross-lingual transfer is less well studied for the language modeling objective, and for modern down-stream evaluation tasks, such as ARC~\cite{clark2018think , HellaSwag~\cite{zellers2019hellaswag , PIQA~\cite{bisk2020piqa , SCIQ~\cite{welbl2017crowdsourcing , WinoGrande~\cite{sakaguchi2021winogrande , etc. \citeauthor{philippy-etal-2023-towards end with a number of recommendations, one of which is to study cross-lingual transfer in more detail for generative models, given their exceptional performance in recent years.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although our work does not directly study cross-lingual transfer (we do not first train on the auxiliary language, and then on the target language), our findings help understand how an auxiliary language can help a target language for which limited data is available.
elements.sent-2411-035: Selecting high quality data for pretraining LLMs remains an active area of research.
elements.sent-2411-036: Early research on data selection was based on heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-037: For example, the original GPT-2 model was pretrained on outbound links filtered from Reddit based on heuristic indicators of whether users found the links interesting, educational, or funny \cite{radford2019language .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Prior approaches also upsampled documents from high quality sources like Wikipedia \cite{gururangan2022whose , or used combinations of heuristics such as absence of stop words, document length, word length, etc. \cite{rae2021scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Other works select data based on quality filters and time of collection \cite{longpre2023pretrainer , model-based quality filtering \cite{sachdeva2024train,li2024datacomp , or textbook quality knowledge \cite{gunasekar2023textbooks,li2023textbooks,kong2024large .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: An alternative approach to data selection is re-weighting data samples to select the best data mixtures for training \cite{fan2023doge,xie2024doremi , or importance sampling based on a downstream task \cite{grangier2024specialized,grangier2024task,xie2023data .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Still a majority of these filtering techniques are applied to English-only datasets, and multilingual datasets such as mC4 have limited data filtering \cite{xue2020mt5 .}
elements.section-2411-003: Using English Data Selection Pipelines to Complement Limited Target Data
elements.sent-2411-042: Existing data selection pipelines have been shown to be effective in monolingual (English) pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-043: We investigate whether these pipelines are useful in the bilingual setup with limited target data.
elements.sent-2411-044: We always take English as the auxiliary language.
elements.sent-2411-045: Initially, we perform all experiments with German as the target language.
elements.sent-2411-046: In Section~\ref{sec:multilingual we discuss how our findings extend across multiple languages.}

elements.section-2411-004: The Effect of Individual Data Transformations
elements.sent-2411-074: Section~\ref{sec:dataset_comparisons investigated the effect of using existing data filtering pipelines in the auxiliary language.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Here we study individual data transformations in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-076: For all experiments, we use the same experimental setup as in Section~\ref{sec:hyperparams .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Unless otherwise stated, we refer to the mC4 datasets as ``Base.''.
elements.sent-2411-078: While filtering strategies achieve strong results on English downstream evaluations, training a filtering model can require more data than available, and typical high quality datasets may not be available in the target language for filtering.
elements.sent-2411-079: Further, it is unclear to what extent better filtering strategies would improve performance in a target language that might not benefit from seeing purely higher quality data in the auxiliary language.
elements.sent-2411-080: To test the impact of filtering, we use the OH+ELI5 fast text classifier to filter data, and filter a large portion of the mC4 to the top 10\% high quality documents following \cite{li2024datacomp .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Filtering with this classifier leads the model to train on high quality English, and question answer style data which specializes the model towards downstream evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-082: We compare the performance of models trained with English filtering and without, holding the German data the same for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-083: We find in Figure~\ref{fig:mb_filter that for the medium models performance improvements in English evaluations are 1.5\%, but for translated German (target) evaluations, there is no improvement.}
elements.sent-2411-084: For the XL models, English evaluations improve by 3\%, but translated German evaluations are under 1\% and within 1 standard error.
elements.sent-2411-085: Prior work shows that LLMs reason in English and that information may be stored in a language agnostic space \cite{wendler2024llamas .}
elements.sent-2411-086: However, they do not control how the information is seen during training, and while much of the information may be seen only in English (the predominant language), it may also be seen in other languages as the pretraining datasets are not made publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-087: Further, we note that FineWeb-EDU, in addition to high quality filtering, also filters for educational quality content \cite{penedo2024fineweb .}
elements.sent-2411-088: To explicitly test whether information is shared between auxiliary and target languages, we upsample topics in English and evaluate on the target language comparing with having uniform data.
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, given access to some small dataset representative of target knowledge, we group the data into target clusters and estimate importance sampling weights over the clusters following \cite{grangier2024specialized .}
elements.sent-2411-090: To train the clustering model, we take a small subset of the training set, produce embeddings from a smaller sentenceBERT model, then cluster the data according to the embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-091: For our importance sampling experiments, we upweight a subset of roughly 300B tokens from the English dataset.
elements.sent-2411-092: Given a small target set (typically on the order of 1000-10000 samples), we assign each sample to a cluster and upweight the original training set based on the cluster assignment proportions.
elements.sent-2411-093: In practice, we do not optimize for the cluster parameters jointly with the model weights, and instead precompute them based on the pretraining and target task data.
elements.sent-2411-094: For clustering hyperparameters, we use a lightweight SentenceTransformers multilingual model\footnote{The particular model is called {\tt paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 model and is obtained from \url{https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: To learn importance sampling weights, we use corresponding training sets, and available corpora that would be used for retrieval-based methods on the same tasks.
elements.sent-2411-096: All upsampling is done in English to facilitate having specialized information in the auxiliary language, but not in the target language to measure the impact of information in a different language.
elements.sent-2411-097: For all experiments, we use the mC4 English dataset.
elements.sent-2411-098: Comparisons are made between having access to more German data of uniform quality, upsampling based on the ARC training set (general science knowledge), and upsampling based on the HellaSwag training set (general knowledge and QA style).
elements.sent-2411-099: Results in Figure~\ref{fig:data_selection show that for the medium model experiments, data selection provides 1.5\% improvement over base mC4 auxiliary data on English language evaluations, but no improvement in the target language (German).}
elements.sent-2411-100: For the XL models, we see 4\% improvement in English evaluations, and 2\% improvement in target language (German).
elements.sent-2411-101: These results highlight that larger models can take advantage of information in the auxiliary language, and the performance improvements are higher for the information upsampling than for model-based filtering without removing the data, allowing for training on new data at greater token counts.

elements.sent-2411-105: While prior work has examined the use of LLM-generated data for pretraining \cite{maini2024rephrasing and finetuning \cite{li2023self,yuan2024self language models, to our knowledge there is no prior work that investigates data selection based on synthetic examples.}}

elements.sent-2411-108: Generating synthetic data using an off-the-shelf language model can be both computationally expensive and challenging, however for the purpose of computing sampling weights, a small number of questions is sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-109: For our experiments, we generate data using a frozen Mistral-7B instruction tuned model\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 \cite{jiang2023mistral .}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: In total around 2000 science question answer pairs (referred to as SciQ) are created with minimal filtering outside of confirming that both a question and answer are specified, and the model does not generate any additional details or explanations.
elements.sent-2411-111: We additionally generate general instruction data.
elements.sent-2411-112: This data is aimed at broad QA style and general fact information which is helpful for downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-113: This data is generated in two stages by first having the frozen Mistral-7B model generate a set of questions, then answering the questions.
elements.sent-2411-114: Using the sets of questions, we identify the cluster sample weights and upsample data accordingly as in Section~\ref{sec:is_information .}
elements.sent-2411-115: The approach otherwise follows the same setup as in Section~\ref{sec:is_information except for the synthetic QA pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Results shown in Figure~\ref{fig:syn_selection comparing synthetic data upsampling with upsampling from the downstream task demonstrates that synthetic data can be sufficient for incorporating information into the auxiliary English dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-117: For the medium models on both English and German evaluations, models trained by upsampling synthetic data and real ARC attain the same performance, and for the XL models, upsampling synthetic data is within 1\% for the English downstream tasks, and within 0.5\% for the translated German evaluations.

elements.section-2411-005: Do Findings Hold Across Multiple Languages?
elements.sent-2411-134: Sections~\ref{sec:exp -\ref{sec:methods_improve focused on German as the target language.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We now investigate to what extent our findings for German hold across different target languages.
elements.sent-2411-136: In Section~\ref{sec:multiple_languages we study additional languages individually, and in Section~\ref{sec:multilingual_mix we investigate a multilingual setting combining a subset of four languages.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: We add seven languages, across four language families: French, Italian, Portuguese, Spanish (Indo-European, same as German), Chinese (Sino-Tibetan), Japanese (Japonic), and Korean (Koreanic) \cite{ethnologue2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: For these experiments, we train models using the same experimental setup as in Section~\ref{sec:exp .}
elements.sent-2411-139: For all additional target language models, we train with approximately 250M tokens from the mC4 corpus in the respective language.
elements.sent-2411-140: For training monolingual models in the target language, we note that the Chinese and Korean mC4 corpora contain fewer than 100B tokens and thus the data is repeated for multiple epochs in the base language experiments.
elements.sent-2411-141: We report results for the XL models trained for 100B tokens in Figure~\ref{fig:multilang_de_fr -\ref{fig:multilang_ja_zh in the appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Results are similar for the medium models.
elements.sent-2411-143: Our results indicate that our findings on English-German training do not extend across all families of languages.
elements.sent-2411-144: In particular, we see improvements from FineWeb-EDU only on the Indo-European languages.
elements.sent-2411-145: We investigate the performance differences further by evaluating the perplexity of models trained on combinations of target language data from mC4 and FineWeb-EDU.
elements.sent-2411-146: We selected four languages (German, French, Chinese, and Japanese) to examine the perplexity across two languages with improved performance from auxiliary data and two languages without.
elements.sent-2411-147: We compare data from the FineWeb-EDU dataset seen during training with data from the mC4 English portion which has not been seen during training.
elements.sent-2411-148: We translate 10,000 documents into each of the target languages and evaluate the perplexity of documents for both translated and original auxiliary data.
elements.sent-2411-149: We additionally report a metric we call exceedance, which is the fraction of documents in FineWeb-EDU with lower perplexity than the average perplexity of documents in mC4 English.

elements.section-2411-006: Scaling Limitations for Low Resource Languages
elements.sent-2411-164: We consider the limitations of data scaling for training bilingual language models for target languages with limited data.
elements.sent-2411-165: In Section~\ref{sec:aux_data_scaling , we investigate the extent to which training on higher quality English data improves the amount of data needed in the target language to reach similar performance.}
elements.sent-2411-166: In Section~\ref{sec:model_size_scaling we investigate how feasible it is to train larger bilingual models with limited target data.}

elements.sent-2411-167: We measure the amount of data needed in the target language to match training on the high quality auxiliary data.
elements.sent-2411-168: Our goal is to quantify the advantage of training models with additional target data beyond the 250M tokens used in prior experiments.
elements.sent-2411-169: For this experiment we train models at different dataset sizes ranging from 0.1B to 100B tokens.
elements.sent-2411-170: We investigate data scaling over both the mC4 dataset and FineWeb-EDU to measure the increase in German data needed to match higher quality auxiliary datasets.
elements.sent-2411-171: Our results are summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:data_scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-172: Models trained on the base mC4 English data achieve similar performance to models trained with only 5B tokens of the base mC4 German data.
elements.sent-2411-173: In contrast, training on a high quality data such as FineWeb-EDU increases the amount of data needed to around between 10-15B tokens or equivalently 2x the amount of data in German.
elements.sent-2411-174: Note that the curves all plateau quickly at around 10B tokens.
elements.sent-2411-175: This corresponds to around 10 repetitions of the data, matching the results in \cite{muennighoff2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-176: There are two important data scaling considerations from Figure~\ref{fig:data_scaling that justify training with auxiliary language data.}
elements.sent-2411-177: First, for languages that are data constrained, it may be infeasible to collect twice as much data.
elements.sent-2411-178: Second, models trained on FineWeb-EDU attain similar performance at a rate of 5x the number of tokens (roughly 50B tokens on FineWebEDU matches the performance of 10B tokens of mC4 German data).
elements.sent-2411-179: An important avenue of research is to investigate data scaling at larger quantities of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-180: In particular, the FineWeb-EDU corpus totals 5.4T tokens and would require access to 1T tokens of German data, which is 3x the amount of data in mC4.
elements.sent-2411-181: As a result, while the data scaling shows large improvements from little German data, the large amount of readily available English data can make training on auxiliary data practical.

elements.sent-2411-182: In this section, we investigate to what extent results scale for training larger language models when data remains constrained in the target language.
elements.sent-2411-183: In prior experiments, we fixed the amount of data from the target language to approximately 250M tokens and train for 100K steps with 5\% of the training being in the target language.
elements.sent-2411-184: However, increasing model size necessitates increasing the number of tokens seen during training according to Chinchilla scaling laws \cite{kaplan2020scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-185: This is challenging for low resource languages, as the number of repetitions increases with increased amount of training, which can lead to overfitting and saturate model performance.
elements.sent-2411-186: We first examine the amount of overfitting larger models have on the same amount of data and ratio of training time.
elements.sent-2411-187: We train a roughly 3B (non-embedding parameter) model consisting of 32 layers, 32 attention heads, and a hidden dimension size of 2560.
elements.sent-2411-188: The model has a maximum sequence length of 2048 and is trained for 150K steps to match the same scaling ratio as for the 300M and 1B models.
elements.sent-2411-189: The perplexity of training and validation data is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:perplexity_overfit for the 1B (left) and 3B models (right).}
elements.sent-2411-190: For 1B models, we see little to no overfitting at 5\% ratio of training steps and the model achieves slightly lower perplexity than a model trained with a lower ratio of 1.5\% target language data.
elements.sent-2411-191: In contrast, for the 3B model, there is clear overfitting from as early as 25\% of the training with 5\% ratio of training steps.
elements.sent-2411-192: This indicates that the number of repetitions is too high for the 3B model and performance may degrade.
elements.sent-2411-193: Reducing the number of repetitions by reducing the fraction of training steps to 1.5\% reduces overfitting as seen in Figure~\ref{fig:overfitting_3B , however perplexity remains similar between the 1B and 3B models when limited to 250M tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-194: Increasing the number of available tokens results in decreasing perplexity on validation data and no overfitting for the 3B model, which is unnecessary for the 1B models.
elements.sent-2411-195: An overview of comparison with different data ratios for zero-shot accuracy is also provided in Appendix~\ref{sec:app_data_ratio .}
elements.sent-2411-196: Based on Figure~\ref{fig:perplexity_overfit , we evaluate both 1B and 3B models with data ratios that mitigate overfitting: 5\% for 1B and 1.25\% for 3B with 250M tokens in the target language.}
elements.sent-2411-198: First, Figure~\ref{fig:1B_3B_250M_005 shows that the 3B model performs similarly to the 1B model with the same 250M amount of target language data matching the similar perplexity values between the two models.}
elements.sent-2411-199: However increasing to 1B tokens results in similar improvements in both English and German when increasing model size from 1B to 3B as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:1B_3B_1B_005 .}
elements.sent-2411-200: Summarizing, there is a limit to the size of models that can be trained with limited target data.
elements.sent-2411-201: Model performance for 250M tokens saturates at 1B parameter models (more than the available data in mC4 for $\sim 20$ languages).
elements.sent-2411-202: Increasing to a 3B parameter model necessitates training on 1B tokens of data (more than the available data in mC4 for $\sim 40$ languages).
elements.sent-2411-203: This work studies how an auxiliary language for which an abundance of training data is available can boost pretraining for a target language for which only limited data is available.
elements.sent-2411-204: We find that adding auxiliary high quality data obtained by data filtering can improve performance in a target language.
elements.sent-2411-205: Moreover, our results indicate that most of these gains can be attributed to having relevant information in the auxiliary language data, which may not be present in the target language.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, we find that results are inconsistent across different target languages.
elements.sent-2411-207: We hypothesize that for languages that are further from English, better English datasets are not as helpful as information is not shared between them.
elements.sent-2411-208: Finally, we find limitations to scaling models for languages that are data constrained.
elements.sent-2411-209: This work takes a step towards pretraining language models in languages with limited data, and can inspire more research into bilingual or multilingual learning under dataset constraints.
elements.sent-2411-210: In this section we list some limitations of our work.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our primary focus is on English-German language training, as these two Germanic family languages share linguistic similarities \cite{ethnologue2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-212: German is one of the most well-represented languages in the mC4 dataset, facilitating model comparisons with varying amounts of German and English data.
elements.sent-2411-213: Furthermore, the availability of extensive public resources for German, including translation systems and translated evaluation data, further supports our emphasis on this language pair.
elements.sent-2411-214: We experimented with seven additional datasets including French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, Korean, Japanese, and Chinese.
elements.sent-2411-215: However, we note that there are many other languages within mC4 and more broadly which can benefit from having auxiliary English data for pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-216: Due to limited evaluation benchmarks and availability of target language data for comparison, we leave investigation for truly low-resource languages to future work.
elements.sent-2411-217: Another limitation in evaluating language models for languages other than English is that many datasets have been translated from English.
elements.sent-2411-218: These datasets may contain cultural biases or information that is not available on the web in other languages.
elements.sent-2411-219: As a result, certain aspects of the evaluation may lead to improved performance when using English auxiliary or translated data.
elements.sent-2411-220: Additionally, translated data often exhibits a distribution different from that of real data in the target languages.
elements.sent-2411-221: Therefore, an important direction for future work is the development of evaluation datasets that are not based on translation, which is essential for more accurate evaluation of multilingual language models.
elements.sent-2411-222: Finally, this work studies three model sizes up to 3B models.
elements.sent-2411-223: We note that there are many standard benchmarks that can be evaluated at 1B-3B scale, however many more benchmarks and patterns can appear at larger model sizes.
elements.sent-2411-224: It is important future work to evaluate whether the results extend to larger scales including evaluating potential ``emergent behaviors'' as well as risks at larger scales \cite{wei2022emergent .}

elements.sent-2411-226: The medium-scale (300M non-embedding parameter) model consists of 24 layers, 16 attention heads, and a hidden dimension size of 1024.
elements.sent-2411-227: The XL-scale (1.3B non-embedding parameter) model consists of 24 layers, 16 attention heads, and a hidden dimension size of 2048.
elements.sent-2411-228: Both models have a maximum sequence length of 1024.
elements.sent-2411-229: The baseline models are trained using NVIDIA’s Megatron-LM\footnote{\url{https://github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM repository for pretraining language models.}}
elements.sent-2411-230: The medium size models are trained for a total of 30K steps, and 100K steps for the XL models at a batch size of 1024.
elements.sent-2411-231: All models are trained using a maximum learning rate of $0.0003$ for the medium model and $0.0002$ for the XL model, and a minimum learning rate of $0.00001$ with a cosine learning rate scheduler and warmup for $1\%$ of the total steps.
elements.sent-2411-232: For regularization, we use a weight decay of $0.01$, along with a gradient clipping norm of $1.0$.
elements.sent-2411-233: Models are trained with the Adam optimizer using $\beta_1=0.9$ and $\beta_2=0.999$.
elements.sent-2411-234: The total training time for XL models on roughly 100B tokens is around 1000 GPUh on Nvidia H100 GPUs.

elements.sent-2411-236: This corpus is derived from CommonCrawl and used for pretraining numerous language models~\cite{brown2020language,raffel2020exploring,touvron2023llama .}
elements.sent-2411-237: The dataset is chosen as all languages have similar data extraction pipelines including line length filter, {\tt cld3 language detection, and deduplication~\cite{xue2020mt5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: The English portion contains 2.7T tokens, German contains 350B tokens, French contains 320B tokens, Spanish contains 430B tokens, Portuguese contains 146B tokens, Italian contains 160B tokens, Korean contains 26B tokens, Japanese contains 160B tokens, and Chinese contains 40B tokens.
elements.sent-2411-239: RedPajamav2: A pretraining corpus with light filtering (primarily only deduplication) comprising 30T tokens and 20T tokens of English text.
elements.sent-2411-240: We focus on the English portion of the dataset only and train using a random shuffled subset of both the head and middle portions~\cite{together2023redpajama .}
elements.sent-2411-241: RefinedWeb: The dataset is also derived from the CommonCrawl, however has a more stringent filtering process including {\tt trafilatura text extraction, document and line level rules, and fuzzy duplication over the original C4 processing~\cite{penedo2023refinedweb .}}
elements.sent-2411-242: FineWeb: This dataset is derived from the CommonCrawl with the aim of replicating RefinedWeb at larger scales.
elements.sent-2411-243: The dataset has some additional filtering including Gopher filtering \cite{rae2021scaling , additional C4 filters, and custom filters for text quality ~\cite{penedo2024fineweb .}}
elements.sent-2411-244: FineWeb-EDU: A subset of the FineWeb dataset which is filtered according to a classifier trained on annotations for educational quality from Llama-3 70B model~\cite{penedo2024fineweb .}
elements.sent-2411-245: SciQ: A dataset of science exam questions, specifically designed to evaluate the ability of NLP models in understanding and reasoning within the scientific domain~\citep{welbl2017crowdsourcing .}
elements.sent-2411-246: ARC Challenge (ARC-C): This dataset is part of the AI2 Reasoning Challenge (ARC)~\citep{clark2018think , containing science exam questions from grades 3 to 9.}
elements.sent-2411-247: The ARC Challenge set includes more difficult questions that necessitate higher-order reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-248: ARC Easy (ARC-E): The Easy set of the AI2 Reasoning Challenge~\citep{clark2018think features questions from the same source as ARC-C but are considered less challenging and do not require as advanced reasoning skills.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Winogrande (Wino.): This dataset challenges models on common sense reasoning in a language context, focusing on pronoun disambiguation tasks~\citep{sakaguchi2021winogrande .}
elements.sent-2411-250: PIQA: Physical Interaction Question Answering tests the understanding of everyday physical processes, an aspect of practical common sense~\citep{bisk2020piqa .}
elements.sent-2411-251: HellaSwag: This dataset evaluates a model's ability to complete scenarios in a contextually and logically coherent manner, requiring both language understanding and common sense reasoning~\citep{zellers2019hellaswag .}

elements.sent-2411-266: The metric utilized for evaluation is the macro token level perplexity.
elements.sent-2411-267: Given a batch of encoded texts, the perplexity at the token level was computed as follows: Given the accumulated loss over the entire dataset, denoted as \( L \), and the total number of tokens, represented by \( T \), the macro token-level perplexity, denoted as \( \mathcal{P \), is calculated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{P} = \exp\left(\min\left(20, \frac{L}{T}\right)\right)\end{equation} Where:.}
elements.sent-2411-268: The value of 20 acts as an upper limit to stabilize the metric in cases of high loss values.
elements.sent-2411-269: For zero-shot MCQ accuracy evaluations, we compute the perplexity of each sentence completion, and choose the lowest perplexity choice.
elements.sent-2411-270: We use the lm-evaluation-harness and where possible evaluate with the length-normalized accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-271: Unless otherwise stated, all evaluations are zero-shot.

elements.subsection-2411-016: Perplexity Evaluations for Translated Training Data
elements.sent-2411-290: In Sections~\ref{sec:multiple_languages and \ref{sec:app_multilang_avg we found that performance trends were not the same across languages.}}
elements.sent-2411-291: In particular, French, German, Portuguese, and Spanish (belonging to the same language family) have similar patterns, however, performance for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean exhibit different patterns.
elements.sent-2411-292: To further test whether the models retain knowledge from one language in another, we translate a small portion of the training set from FineWeb-EDU and mC4 English totaling 10,000 documents.
elements.sent-2411-293: We then translate the data using the v3 translation system from Section~\ref{sec:translation .}
elements.sent-2411-294: We measure both the macro perplexity of all documents as well as the fraction of times where the translated and original data from FineWeb-EDU (training set) have lower loss than the average loss of documents from mC4 English (not part of the training set but from a similar distribution).
elements.sent-2411-295: We refer to this quantity as translated and original exceedance.
elements.sent-2411-296: Having lower loss means the data is more familiar to the model, and having an equal exceedance across the original and translated data means the model can reason equally in either language.
elements.sent-2411-297: Our results are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:multilingual_perp .}
elements.sent-2411-298: We find that perplexity is nearly identical for original data, but much higher for translated data in all languages.
elements.sent-2411-299: For exceedance, in English, we see that the scores are all around 80\%.
elements.sent-2411-300: However, we see that for Japanese and Chinese these values are much lower, indicating that seeing the data in English for these languages does not lower the perplexity in the target language and that the model is not making use of information in the other language.

elements.sent-2411-306: In this work, we study how to boost pretrained model performance in a target language with insufficient pretraining data for training a high performing language model, by enlisting data from an auxiliary language for which high quality data is available.
elements.sent-2411-307: We study this by quantifying the performance gap between training with data in a data-rich auxiliary language compared with training in the target language, exploring the benefits of translation systems, studying the limitations of model scaling when data is limited in the target languages, and proposing new methods for upsampling data from the auxiliary language.
elements.sent-2411-308: The abundance of high quality English data has given rise to exceptional language modeling performance in English~\citep{brown2020language,bubeck2023sparks, OpenAI2023GPT4TR .}
elements.sent-2411-309: However, there are many other languages for which a reasonably large amount of data is available, yet not in equally large amounts as for English.
elements.sent-2411-310: Taking mC4~\cite{xue2020mt5 as an example, languages like German, French, Chinese, and Japanese have $\sim$10-100 times less data than English.}
